message.content:
## Web Searcher: formant-synth-vs-neural/blind-power-users

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Your angle: **formant-synth-vs-neural/blind-power-users** — Covers angle 2: practitioner/community knowledge plus any research on why formant synthesizers beat natural neural voices at extreme rates; intelligibility-vs-naturalness trade-off.
Search query: `blind screen reader users prefer Eloquence DECtalk eSpeak formant synthesis over neural TTS fast speech intelligibility 300 words per minute`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Links: [{"title":"The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users","url":"https://vuink.com/post/fghss-d-dvagreserr-d-dpn/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders-d-dhtml"},{"title":"Information Wayfinding of Screen Reader Users: Five Personas to Expand Conceptualizations of User Experiences - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11872227/"},{"title":"The voice behind screen readers | Fable","url":"https://makeitfable.com/article/the-voice-behind-screen-readers/"},{"title":"Screen Readers | American Foundation for the Blind","url":"https://afb.org/blindness-and-low-vision/using-technology/assistive-technology-products/screen-readers"},{"title":"screen reader and Speech output - why is it so fast and sounds artificial? - Accessibility Consulting - Training & Support","url":"https://www.netz-barrierefrei.de/en/screenreader-output.html"},{"title":"Addressing concerns about CSS Speech - Tink - Léonie Watson","url":"https://tink.uk/addressing-concerns-about-css-speech/"},{"title":"ReadSpeaker vs. Screen Readers: How Are They Different? - ReadSpeaker","url":"https://www.readspeaker.com/blog/readspeaker-vs-screen-readers/"},{"title":"Text to Speech Reader for the Blind | Speechify","url":"https://speechify.com/blog/text-to-speech-reader-blind/"},{"title":"Screen reader with focus-based speech verbosity","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/9575624"}]

Based on the search results, here's what research shows about why blind users prefer Eloquence and similar voices over natural-sounding ones:

## Key Reasons for Preference

Blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient, unlike sighted users who prefer voices that are natural, conversational, and as human-like as possible.

This preference results in blind users favoring voices that sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. For comparison, the speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute.

## Why High Speech Rate Matters

The needs of a blind text to speech user are vastly different than those of a sighted user. Screen reader users rely on audio as their primary means of accessing digital content, so efficiency and speed are critical. For quicker access, most screen reader users set their voice to high speech rates of 500 or more words per minute.

## Eloquence's Popularity

By far the most popular speech synthesizer is Eloquence. It's rather robotic sounding, but has just enough human qualities to be pleasant to the ear, as well as understood at a high rate of speech. Eloquence is the voice preferred by the majority of western English blind users.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about blind screen reader users' preferences regarding different text-to-speech synthesizers:

## Key Findings:

**Formant Synthesis Preference:** Blind users find formant synths easier to comprehend when speeded up than concatenated ones. This indicates an advantage for older formant-based synthesizers like Eloquence when processing speech at accelerated rates.

**Eloquence Preference:** The Eloquence TTS voice was the single most mentioned element of preference by respondents in a study of vision-impaired screen reader users. Additionally, eSpeak TTS on NVDA was noted repeatedly as being mechanical sounding and unattractive, though there was very little discussion on the intelligibility of eSpeak.

**Individual Variation:** One experienced blind screen reader user reported that if listening to DECtalk or ESpeak, for example, or a synth created using a human voice, they can't go as fast compared to Eloquence, though if used long enough, certainly speed would improve.

However, the search results do not contain specific information comparing formant synthesis performance to neural TTS at extreme speeds like 300 words per minute, or detailed scientific studies quantifying intelligibility differences at ultra-fast speeds between these synthesis methods.

PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"(PDF) Why do blind listeners use visual cortex for understanding ultra‐fast speech?","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/224881917_Why_do_blind_listeners_use_visual_cortex_for_understanding_ultra-fast_speech"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study | BMC Neuroscience | Full Text","url":"https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24167485/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar - a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23879896/"},{"title":"Frontiers | Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full"},{"title":"Network Modeling for Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) Signals during Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Late-Blind Listeners","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4492787/"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PMC","url":"https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3805979"}]

I found several research studies on ultrafast speech comprehension in blind listeners. Here are the key findings:

## Speech Comprehension Rates

Individuals suffering from vision loss of a peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second - exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s).

## Neural Mechanisms

The studies used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to investigate how blind individuals' brains process time-compressed speech. Blind subjects were studied while listening to sentence utterances of a moderately fast (8 syl/s) or ultra-fast (16 syl/s) syllabic rate.

Blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv), in addition to left inferior frontal gyrus (IFG), bilateral posterior superior temporal sulcus (pSTS) and left supplementary motor area (SMA).

## Plasticity and Training

Previous findings indicate the central-visual system to contribute to the processing of accelerated speech in blind subjects. A training study addressed whether acquisition of ultra-fast (18 syl/s) speech perception skills induces de novo central-visual hemodynamic activation in late-blind participants.

The research demonstrates remarkable cross-modal plasticity, where blind individuals recruit visual cortex regions to enhance their auditory processing of time-compressed speech.

Links: [{"title":"Revisiting the discussion on large language model based screen readers for everyday use. | AppleVis","url":"https://www.applevis.com/forum/assistive-technology/revisiting-discussion-large-language-model-based-screen-readers-everyday"},{"title":"The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users · espeak-ng · Discussion #2338","url":"https://github.com/orgs/espeak-ng/discussions/2338"},{"title":"Complete Guide to Text-to-Speech (TTS) Technology (2025)","url":"https://picovoice.ai/blog/complete-guide-to-text-to-speech/"},{"title":"Reduced latency text-to-speech system","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/9646601"},{"title":"Beyond Unified Models: A Service-Oriented Approach to Low Latency, Context Aware Phonemization for Real Time TTS","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2512.08006v1"},{"title":"9 Best Open Source TTS Engines for Voice Synthesis - Code B","url":"https://code-b.dev/blog/open-source-text-to-speech-tts-engines"},{"title":"Synthesizers - tecwindow site","url":"https://tecwindow.net/en/?sub=54"},{"title":"The best text to speech AI models in 2026 - Fingoweb","url":"https://www.fingoweb.com/blog/the-best-text-to-speech-ai-models-in-2026/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about modern AI neural TTS compared to traditional systems for screen reader users:

## Modern vs. Traditional TTS for Screen Readers

The past year has seen an explosion in new text to speech engines based on neural networks, large language models, and machine learning, but the question of whether this advancement has offered anything to screen reader users remains.

## Formant Synthesis and Traditional Approaches

Formant synthesis models are generally tiny, very flexible, and intelligible at high speeds, however, they often sound robotic and it's difficult to achieve naturalness. Formant-synthesis-based voices like Eloquence and ESpeak are capable of creating natural speech at fairly low latency.

## ESpeak and Eloquence

ESpeak is still widely used for accessibility despite having more robotic voice quality compared to modern neural TTS engines; it's a lightweight engine with a small footprint that runs efficiently on low-power devices. Eloquence Text-To-Speech synthesizer is available as an Android ported version.

## Modern Neural Approaches

For real-time voice generation, models like ElevenLabs, OpenAI, and Azure TTS are excellent choices that support low-latency audio streaming. Recent neural TTS systems like VITS and Piper offer good quality with low-latency through acoustic modeling and vocaloding.

The key tension appears to be between the proven speed and consistency of traditional formant synthesizers versus the improved naturalness of modern neural systems, particularly regarding latency concerns for screen reader users.

Redirect URL: https://idp.springer.com/authorize?response_type=cookie&client_id=springerlink&redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Flink.springer.com%2Farticle%2F10.1186%2F1471-2202-14-74

To complete your request, I need to fetch content from the redirected URL. Please use WebFetch again with these parameters:
- url: "https://idp.springer.com/authorize?response_type=cookie&client_id=springerlink&redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Flink.springer.com%2Farticle%2F10.1186%2F1471-2202-14-74"
- prompt: "Extract the key quantitative findings about ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind listeners: maximum syllables per second blind vs sighted listeners can comprehend, the syllabic rates tested. Capture any numbers on comprehension rates."

# Why Blind Screen Reader Users Prefer Formant Synthesizers Over Neural TTS

## Preferences for Traditional Systems

According to the article, blind users favor voices that are "fast, clear, predictable, and efficient" rather than natural-sounding. The author notes that preferred voices "sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute," compared to average human speech at 200-250 WPM.

## Shortcomings of Modern AI/Neural TTS

The author identifies four critical problems with contemporary AI-based text-to-speech models when applied to screen readers:

**1. Accuracy Issues**
Modern systems "had a tendency to skip words, read numbers incorrectly, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints." For screen reader users, this is unacceptable since "skipping words, or reading numbers incorrectly, is unacceptable."

**2. Speed/Latency Problems**
Unlike older systems, "Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text." Screen reader users need systems that "quickly discard and restart speech" when navigating rapidly.

**3. Control Limitations**
Traditional systems allow customization of "pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize, and other parameters." AI models cannot offer this due to being "trained on data from a particular set of speakers," resulting in "a loss of functionality that many blind users depend on."

**4. Dependency Bloat**
Neural models require 30-103+ Python packages, slowing NVDA's startup and introducing security vulnerabilities.

content.text: I have excellent detail from the practitioner article. The Springer link requires auth, but I already have the key figures from the PMC version (22 syl/s blind vs 8 syl/s sighted) from the earlier search. I have enough to deliver high-quality ranked results.

results.title: The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (Samuel Proulx / eSpeak-NG discussion, Jan 2026)
results.snippet: The single most directly on-point source for angle 2. The author (a blind power user) and the eSpeak-NG community lay out exactly WHY formant synths beat neural TTS at speed. Full article (stuff.interfree.ca/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders.html) gives four concrete failure modes of neural/AI TTS for fast screen-reader use: (1) ACCURACY — neural models skip words, misread numbers, chop off short utterances, ignore prosody hints (unacceptable when scanning); (2) LATENCY — models like Supertonic/Kitten TTS can't start until they have a whole text chunk, so they can't instantly discard-and-restart on rapid navigation the way formant synths can; (3) CONTROL — formant synths expose pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize as live knobs, neural models trained on fixed speakers can't; (4) DEPENDENCY BLOAT — neural models pull 30-103+ Python packages, slowing NVDA startup. Confirms formant voices are 'tiny, very flexible, intelligible at high speeds' and that Eloquence/eSpeak run at low latency. Directly relevant to the local/on-device Kokoro constraint.

results.title: The State of Modern AI Text-To-Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (full article, Samuel Proulx)
results.snippet: The full text behind the eSpeak-NG discussion. States blind users prefer voices that are 'fast, clear, predictable, and efficient' over natural-sounding ones, understood 'upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute' vs 200-250 WPM for normal human speech. Quantifies the intelligibility-vs-naturalness trade-off as a practitioner sees it and explains the consistency/crispness/predictability advantages that make older formant synths the power-user choice. Useful for distinguishing the 'aggressively speed-optimized track' from the natural default voice in the user's two-track pipeline.

results.title: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar — an fMRI study (BMC Neuroscience, 2013)
results.snippet: Peer-reviewed neuroscience providing the hard numbers for the original question's 18-22 syl/s claim. Blind listeners can comprehend speech up to ~22 syllables/second vs ~8 syl/s ceiling for normal-sighted listeners. Tested moderately-fast (8 syl/s) vs ultra-fast (16 syl/s) rates. Shows the comprehension ceiling is a trainable neural-plasticity phenomenon (cross-modal recruitment of V1/fusiform/pulvinar), which explains why only 'trained power listeners' can use the aggressive track — grounding the user's two-audience design decision in evidence.

results.title: Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans (Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013)
results.snippet: Companion training study: late-blind participants acquired ultra-fast (18 syl/s) speech-perception skills, and the skill induced de novo visual-cortex activation. Demonstrates the high-speed comprehension is a learned/trainable ability rather than innate — directly supports the idea that an 'optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners' is viable and that comprehensibility at extreme rates improves with exposure. Reinforces measuring gains against a trained-listener population.

results.title: The 'voice' has it: screen reader adoption and switching behavior among vision-impaired persons (PMC)
results.snippet: Empirical user study where the Eloquence TTS voice was 'the single most mentioned element of preference' among vision-impaired screen-reader users, and a key statement that blind users find FORMANT synths easier to comprehend when sped up than CONCATENATED ones — directly supporting the synthesis-method intelligibility argument. eSpeak on NVDA was called mechanical/unattractive but intelligibility was rarely questioned. Provides survey-grade evidence for the formant-preference claim beyond anecdote.

results.title: Extraordinary Ability of Blind People to Hear Ultrafast Speech (R. Douglas Fields, Scientific American contributor)
results.snippet: Accessible science-journalism summary of the ultra-fast-speech research, useful for framing the phenomenon (blind listeners parsing speech far faster than sighted listeners) for a general/practitioner audience. Good background bridge between the peer-reviewed fMRI papers and the engineering recommendations; lower relevance than primary sources but helpful context on why intelligibility at extreme rates matters and is achievable.

content.text:
I've completed the research on the formant-synth-vs-neural / blind-power-users angle and returned six ranked results via StructuredOutput. Key findings surfaced:

- **Practitioner core (high relevance):** The eSpeak-NG community discussion and Samuel Proulx's full article give the most direct answer to angle 2 — four concrete reasons formant synths beat neural TTS at speed (accuracy/no word-skipping, instant discard-restart latency, live control knobs like pitch/headsize/breathiness, and minimal dependency footprint), plus the 800-900 WPM power-user figure vs 200-250 WPM normal speech.
- **Scientific grounding (high relevance):** The BMC Neuroscience fMRI study quantifies the ceiling (~22 syl/s for trained blind listeners vs ~8 syl/s for sighted), and the Frontiers training study shows it's a learnable plasticity effect — directly justifying the user's "optional aggressive track for trained listeners" design.
- **Survey evidence (medium):** The PMC adoption study confirms Eloquence is the most-preferred voice and that formant synths are easier to comprehend when sped up than concatenated voices.

All findings are in the StructuredOutput call above.
